Student & Parent Enrollment Handbook

learn songs.
Become a musician.

Guitar · Piano · Vocals · Ukulele · Drums · Violin Home · Online · Centre · Mentorship
Scan to enquire
The problem

Most students don’t stop because of talent. They stop because there was never a system.

Most learners begin with real excitement. A few months in, they can play a song or two — but something is missing.

  • Rhythm that isn’t quite steady
  • Technique that was never built properly
  • No clear idea of what comes next
  • Practice that drifts without a task
  • Confidence that never reaches a stage

Lessons happen. Songs get taught. But there’s no map — no record of what was covered, no goal for the month, no way for a parent to see whether real progress is happening underneath the songs.

The result is a familiar story: months of classes, a small pile of half-songs, and a quiet decision to “take a break.”

“The missing piece is almost never talent. It’s structure.”

Musicphonetics was built to replace that guesswork with a system — teaching, tracking, accountability and performance, working together.

Musicphonetics · Enrollment Handbook01
From the founder
Abhishek Kumar Founder · Musicphonetics

Guitarist, vocalist and music educator · 10+ years teaching · 1,100+ one-to-one students · invited as a judge at colleges across Delhi NCR.

It began in 2015 with one guitar, three chords and one student.

There was no office and no company — only a conviction that a learner given the right start could carry music for the rest of their life.

After more than eleven hundred one-to-one students — children, teenagers and adults — one pattern became impossible to ignore. The problem was never the teachers. The same lesson could transform one student and lose another — not because of skill, but because of structure.

So I built the structure I always wished existed: a clear pathway, honest progress that parents can actually see, teachers who are supported and accountable, and real stages to play on. Not songs handed out at random — a way to genuinely become a musician.

I know what it means for a family to invest in their child’s music. This handbook is my promise that the investment is met with a real system — and real care.

“Every learner deserves a teacher, a structure and a path.”

— Abhishek Kumar

From the founder02
What is Musicphonetics

A complete music-education ecosystem — not a single weekly class.

Musicphonetics brings together excellent teachers, personalised lessons, technology, accountability and performance — so learning keeps moving between one class and the next.

Teaching

Weekly guided one-to-one lessons, structured goals and real practice support between classes.

Accountability

Attendance, homework and teacher observations recorded — never left to memory.

Visibility

Parents see progress, goals, reports and payments through a private portal.

The ecosystem03
What makes us different

Ordinary lessons vs. a real system.

This isn’t about any individual teacher. It’s about whether learning sits inside a structure — or depends on memory and goodwill.

Ordinary music tuitionThe Musicphonetics system
Classes happen informallyClasses follow a structured method
Progress depends on memoryProgress is recorded and visible
Parents get limited visibilityParents view attendance, homework & reports
Songs are taught at randomTechnique, theory, musicianship & repertoire develop together
Teacher quality variesTeachers pass a structured onboarding standard
No clear monthly goalGoals and milestones are defined
Missed classes create confusionAttendance & scheduling are documented
Learning ends inside the lessonPractice & homework continue between lessons
Little performance exposureStudents build toward the stage
Fees handled informally, in cashPayments handled securely in the company’s name

Many wonderful independent teachers exist. The difference here is the system around the teacher — the part that keeps a learner progressing for years, not months.

Ordinary vs. a system04

The learning journey05
How we develop complete musicians

Music is more than songs.

A real musician is built from many strands at once. We develop them deliberately — not by accident.

Technique

Clean, healthy playing built on proper fundamentals.

Rhythm

Steady, confident time — the backbone of musicality.

Listening

An ear that recognises pitch, harmony and feel.

Repertoire

Songs and pieces that grow in range and difficulty.

Music theory

Understanding why music works, not just copying it.

Creativity

Improvising, arranging and making music your own.

Confidence

The quiet certainty that comes from real competence.

Performance

Playing for others — the skill that ties it all together.

Practice discipline

Consistent, purposeful practice that compounds over time.

Complete musicians06
Who we teach

We start with the need — not the age.

A four-year-old, a nervous teenager and an adult returning after twenty years each need something different. We don’t sort people into age boxes. We understand the exact need — then build the lesson around it.

Age tells us very little on its own. What actually shapes a great lesson is the answer to a few honest questions:

  • What does this learner want from music — joy, mastery, a stage, an exam, calm?
  • What have they tried before, and what got in the way?
  • How much time can they realistically give to practice?
  • Do they learn best by ear, by reading, by watching, by playing?
  • What will keep this person coming back next week?

The answers set the pace, the repertoire, the amount of theory and the kind of encouragement — for a young child and a working adult alike.

The same principle, tailored
  • Young children — rhythm, listening, movement and joyful familiarity, with a parent gently involved.
  • School-age learners — foundations, technique and their first real songs, with practice habits that stick.
  • Teenagers — their own musical identity: favourite genres, creativity, performance, exams or ambition.
  • Adults — flexible goals, whether that’s unwinding, finally learning properly, or preparing to perform.
  • Returning learners — an honest reset that respects what they already know.

Expectations are always set to fit the learner — never a one-size promise.

“Tell us the need. We’ll build the music around it.”
Need, not age07
Programmes & instruments

What you can learn.

We only advertise an instrument when we can genuinely match a suitable teacher. Availability is confirmed at enquiry.

Guitar

From first chords to full songs.

Piano / Keyboard

Melody, harmony and reading.

Vocals

Healthy technique — Western & Hindustani.

Ukulele

An easy, joyful start to music.

Drums / Cajon

Rhythm, groove and coordination.

Violin

Tone, intonation and classical foundations.

Learning paths
  • One-to-One Lessons — our primary route, built entirely around one learner.
  • Trinity Preparation — structured support for graded exams, woven into the method.
  • The Director’s Circle — premium, faculty-led learning by prior booking.
Growing with demand
  • Group & Academy batches — opening as demand, location and teacher availability allow.
  • Workshops & camps — seasonal intensives and performance preparation.
Founder decision — Confirm whether to list additional instruments (e.g. flute, saxophone, tabla) only once a verified teacher is available.

Student plans10
Inside the student experience

One class. A complete ecosystem.

A weekly lesson is the centre of a much larger cycle. Here’s everything that surrounds it.

Before
  • Class prepared to the plan
  • Last week’s homework reviewed
  • The month’s goal in focus
During
  • Live, structured instruction
  • Technique, theory & repertoire together
  • Teacher observations noted
After
  • Attendance updated
  • Practice & homework assigned
  • Progress & notes visible to parents
The lesson ends. The learning doesn’t.

This is the difference between “attending classes” and being carried through a genuine learning cycle, week after week.

The parent portal12
Progress you can see

Real progress, made visible.

On the Foundation pathway, a learner moves through a 32-class beginner journey in four chapters — and you can watch it advance.

1

Starting Right

Sound, rhythm, posture and a practice routine.

2

Building Basics

Notes, chords, steady rhythm and a first song section.

3

Musical Confidence

Applying basics into songs, timing and independence.

4

Ready for Next Level

A performance piece and readiness for the Main Pathway.

S-05 · Curriculum progressThe Foundation progress bar & current chapter
Milestones update as classes are completed — progress is derived from real attendance, not guesswork.

Musical growth isn’t perfectly linear, and we don’t promise fixed results by a fixed week. What we promise is that progress is tracked honestly — so you always know where things stand.

Accuracy note — We describe identity and payment verification and safeguarding acknowledgement, which the system performs. We do not claim police or formal background checks unless those are actually completed.
Our teachers14
The first 90 days

What a well-supported start can look like.

Every learner is different. This is a considered rhythm — not a guarantee of identical outcomes.

Month 1

Understanding the learner

  • Level & needs assessment
  • Goal setting
  • Building a routine
  • Teacher–student connection
Month 2

Building foundations

  • Technique
  • Rhythm
  • First repertoire
  • Practice consistency
Month 3

Visible direction

  • Growing confidence
  • Clear learning goals
  • Progress review
  • Next-stage planning

Pace depends on the need, consistency, programme and previous experience. We’d rather set an honest expectation than an impressive one.

The first 90 days15
Practice that actually works

Short, regular, purposeful.

Progress lives between the lessons. A few honest principles matter more than long, rare sessions:

  • Short and regular beats long and occasional.
  • Every practice should have a clear task, not just “play something.”
  • Repetition must stay purposeful — quality over quantity.
  • Write down questions to bring to the next class.
  • Encourage without creating fear. Consistency is the real skill.
Sample weekly practice

New / young learners

10–15 min, most days · with a parent nearby · one small task per day.

Developing learners

20–30 min, 4–5 days · warm-up, technique, then the song.

Committed / adult learners

30–45 min, most days · goal-led, with focused problem-solving.

Your teacher tailors these to the learner’s real need and schedule.

Practice that works16
The parent’s role

Your support changes everything.

You don’t need to be musical. You just need to protect the conditions for progress.

Helpful

  • Protect the class time
  • Provide a quiet space to practise
  • Encourage consistency, gently
  • Glance at the homework
  • Communicate concerns early
  • Celebrate small progress
  • Let the child find their own voice

Unhelpful

  • Frequent cancellations
  • Comparing to other children
  • Expecting songs in week one
  • Practising only before class
  • Pressure that turns into fear
  • Bypassing the teacher’s guidance
  • Disorganised scheduling & payments

None of this is about blame. It’s simply what, in our experience, helps a learner flourish.

The parent’s role17
Performance, confidence & community

Practice becomes real on a stage.

Performance is where confidence, preparation, discipline and expression finally come together.

Running now
  • Open Mic & Chai — a warm, low-pressure stage for students to perform.
  • Student showcases and recital moments.
Growing
  • Annual concerts and larger showcases
  • Recordings and creative projects
  • Ensembles and peer learning
  • Community collaborations

We describe current opportunities honestly and label future initiatives as growing plans — never as guarantees.

Confidence isn’t taught. It’s earned — one performance at a time.
Performance & community18
Safety, trust & professionalism

Every lesson should feel safe.

  • Child safety and appropriate teacher conduct
  • Clear professional boundaries
  • Parent visibility of learning
  • Approved communication channels
  • Confidentiality & responsible data handling
  • Secure, private portal access
  • A clear way to raise concerns
  • Transparent, company-managed payments

“Every learning relationship should feel safe, respectful, transparent and professionally supported.”

Safety & trust19
Payments & transparency

Clear, secure, in the company’s name.

  • Families pay Musicphonetics directly — securely, in the company’s name.
  • Teachers do not privately collect fees. If anyone asks you to, please tell us.
  • Invoices and receipts are provided where applicable, and visible in your portal.
  • Your learning records stay connected to the company system, not a private arrangement.
How you pay

Payments are handled through approved, secure company channels. New enrolments and renewals are confirmed with a receipt, and dues appear clearly in the parent portal.

Secure company payments

This protects your family, protects our teachers, and keeps every rupee accounted for.

Payments & transparency20

Real stories21
Why begin now

The best time to begin well is now.

  • Habits form more easily when they start with structure.
  • Confidence grows through guided, consistent practice.
  • The right teacher and system can save months of frustration.
  • Preferred schedules, locations and teachers do fill up.

There’s no countdown here and no pressure. The urgency is simply this: a strong beginning is worth far more than a rushed one.

We’d rather you start well than start today.

Why begin now22
